expanded_description: Mastering titration calculations—applying stoichiometric principles to determine unknown concentrations from titration data, including acid-base, redox, and back-titration scenarios with proper significant figures and error analysis.

core_concept.principle: Titration calculations are based on stoichiometric equivalence: at the endpoint, the moles of titrant and analyte are related by the balanced chemical equation, enabling calculation of unknown concentration from measured volumes and known concentration.
core_concept.explanation: The fundamental titration equation relates moles of titrant to moles of analyte through stoichiometric coefficients: $\dfrac{M_{\text{analyte}}V_{\text{analyte}}}{a} = \dfrac{M_{\text{titrant}}V_{\text{titrant}}}{b}$ where $a$ and $b$ are stoichiometric coefficients from the balanced equation. This relationship, combined with precise volume measurements, enables accurate determination of unknown concentrations in diverse analytical applications.

image.alt: Diagram showing titration calculation workflow from volumes and concentration to unknown concentration
image.caption: Titration calculations use stoichiometry to convert titrant data to analyte concentration

sections.introduction: All titration calculations stem from one fundamental principle: at the equivalence point, the reactants are present in stoichiometric proportions according to the balanced chemical equation. Understanding and correctly applying this relationship is the key to accurate titration analysis.

key_concept.definition: The fundamental titration equation is $\dfrac{M_{\text{A}}V_{\text{A}}}{a} = \dfrac{M_{\text{B}}V_{\text{B}}}{b}$ where A = analyte, B = titrant, and $a$ and $b$ are stoichiometric coefficients from the balanced equation.
key_concept.context: This equation applies to all titration types (acid-base, redox, precipitation, complexometric) as long as the reaction stoichiometry is known and the endpoint accurately reflects the equivalence point.

principles.statement: At the equivalence point, moles of analyte and titrant are related by stoichiometric coefficients: $\dfrac{n_{\text{A}}}{a} = \dfrac{n_{\text{B}}}{b}$.
principles.application: Since $n = M \times V$, this becomes $\dfrac{M_{\text{A}}V_{\text{A}}}{a} = \dfrac{M_{\text{B}}V_{\text{B}}}{b}$, enabling calculation of any unknown variable.
principles.analogy: Like a recipe ratio—if a recipe needs 2 eggs per 1 cup flour, knowing flour amount tells you exactly how many eggs you need.

principles.statement: Molarity relates moles to volume: $n = M \times V$, enabling conversion between measurable volumes and chemical amounts.
principles.application: Always convert volumes to liters before using in molarity calculations, or ensure consistent units throughout.
principles.example: 25.00 mL of 0.100 M NaOH contains $0.02500 \text{ L} \times 0.100 \text{ mol/L} = 0.00250 \text{ mol}$ NaOH
principles.analogy: Like converting currency—molarity is the exchange rate between volume (mL) and chemical amount (mol).

components.definition: Titration calculations involve four variables: $M_{\text{analyte}}$, $V_{\text{analyte}}$, $M_{\text{titrant}}$, $V_{\text{titrant}}$, plus stoichiometric coefficients.
components.interpretation: Given any three variables plus stoichiometry, the fourth can be calculated; typically $M_{\text{titrant}}$, $V_{\text{titrant}}$, and $V_{\text{analyte}}$ are known, solving for $M_{\text{analyte}}$.

image.alt: Diagram showing the four titration variables and their relationships
image.caption: Four variables linked by stoichiometry enable calculation of unknown concentration

worked_examples.title: Basic 1:1 Titration Calculation
worked_examples.problem: A 25.00 mL sample of HCl solution requires 22.45 mL of 0.1015 M NaOH to reach the endpoint. Calculate the concentration of HCl. Reaction: $\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

step_by_step.explanation: From balanced equation: 1 mol HCl reacts with 1 mol NaOH
All measurements have 4 sig figs; report answer with 4 sig figs

worked_examples.concept_applied: For 1:1 stoichiometry, $M_{\text{A}}V_{\text{A}} = M_{\text{B}}V_{\text{B}}$; volumes can remain in mL as long as consistent.

worked_examples.title: Titration with Non-1:1 Stoichiometry
worked_examples.problem: A 20.00 mL sample of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ solution requires 32.50 mL of 0.150 M NaOH to reach the endpoint. Calculate the concentration of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$. Reaction: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

step_by_step.explanation: From balanced equation: 1 mol $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ reacts with 2 mol NaOH
Calculate moles NaOH, convert to moles $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, divide by volume

worked_examples.concept_applied: Non-1:1 stoichiometry requires accounting for coefficients; polyprotic acids need careful mole ratio application.

sections.key_concept: A systematic approach to titration calculations prevents errors and ensures consistent, accurate results. Following these steps for every titration problem builds reliable problem-solving habits.

process_flow.process: Write and Balance the Chemical Equation
process_flow.description: Identify reactants, products, and stoichiometric coefficients
process_flow.mechanism: Balanced equation provides mole ratio essential for all subsequent calculations
Clear identification prevents confusion about which values to use
process_flow.result: Organized problem setup with all variables labeled

process_flow.process: Convert Volumes to Liters (Optional but Recommended)
process_flow.description: Convert mL to L for molarity calculations: mL ÷ 1000 = L
process_flow.result: Volumes in liters ready for $n = M \times V$ calculations

process_flow.mechanism: Converts measurable volume to chemical amount (moles)
process_flow.result: Moles of titrant at equivalence point

process_flow.description: Convert moles titrant to moles analyte using coefficients from balanced equation

process_flow.mechanism: Converts moles back to concentration using sample volume

process_flow.description: Round to appropriate sig figs; verify answer is chemically reasonable
process_flow.mechanism: Match precision to least precise measurement; check magnitude makes sense
process_flow.result: Final answer with proper precision and validated accuracy

worked_examples.title: Complete Step-by-Step Titration Calculation
worked_examples.problem: A 15.00 mL sample of phosphoric acid ($\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$) is titrated with 0.100 M NaOH. The endpoint requires 42.30 mL of NaOH. Calculate the concentration of $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$. Reaction: $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_3\text{PO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$

step_by_step.calculation: $n_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4} = 0.004230 \text{ mol NaOH} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol H}_3\text{PO}_4}{3 \text{ mol NaOH}} = 0.001410 \text{ mol}$

step_by_step.explanation: All measurements have 3-4 sig figs; report with 3 sig figs (limited by 0.100 M)

worked_examples.concept_applied: 7-step systematic method ensures accurate titration calculations with proper stoichiometry and significant figures.

sections.key_concept: Not all titrations follow the simple direct titration pattern. Understanding special scenarios like back-titrations, dilutions, and polyprotic acids enables accurate analysis of complex samples.

classifications.value: Used when direct titration is impractical due to slow reaction, insolubility, or volatile analyte
characteristics: Add excess standard reagent to analyte
Titrate unreacted excess with second standard
classifications.behavior: Analyte moles = total reagent moles - excess reagent moles (from back-titration)

examples.explanation: Add excess HCl to tablet; antacid neutralizes some HCl; titrate remaining HCl with NaOH; calculate acid-neutralizing capacity by difference
Add excess HCl to $\text{CaCO}_3$; $\text{CO}_2$ evolves; titrate excess HCl with NaOH; calculate $\text{CaCO}_3$ content

classifications.value: When sample is diluted before titration, final concentration must be corrected for dilution factor
characteristics: Titration gives concentration of diluted sample
Multiply by dilution factor for original concentration

examples.explanation: Dilute 10.00 mL vinegar to 100.0 mL; titrate 25.00 mL aliquot; dilution factor = $(100.0/10.00) = 10$; multiply titration result by 10
Dilute concentrated acid for safe handling; apply dilution factor to obtain original concentration

classifications.value: Acids with multiple ionizable protons may show multiple equivalence points
characteristics: Each proton titrates separately if $K_a$ values differ by >$10^3$
Stoichiometry depends on how many protons titrate
classifications.behavior: Total moles $\text{H}^+$ = moles base at final equivalence point; intermediate endpoints indicate partial neutralization

examples.explanation: Three protons; if titrated to third equivalence point: 1 mol $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ : 3 mol NaOH; if only to first endpoint: 1:1 ratio
First proton strong (complete), second proton weak ($K_{a2} = 1.2 \times 10^{-2}$); typically titrate both protons with strong base

classifications.value: Titration determines amount of pure substance in impure sample
characteristics: Calculate moles of pure substance from titration
Convert to mass using molar mass
Divide by sample mass and multiply by 100%
classifications.behavior: $\% \text{ purity} = \dfrac{\text{mass of pure substance}}{\text{mass of sample}} \times 100\%$

examples.explanation: Titrate impure KHP with NaOH; calculate pure KHP mass from titration; report % purity
Dissolve ore; reduce all Fe to $\text{Fe}^{2+}$; titrate with $\text{KMnO}_4$; calculate % Fe in ore

worked_examples.title: Back-Titration Calculation
worked_examples.problem: A 0.500 g antacid tablet is dissolved and treated with 50.00 mL of 0.100 M HCl. After reaction, the excess HCl requires 18.35 mL of 0.1015 M NaOH for neutralization. Calculate the acid-neutralizing capacity in moles HCl neutralized per gram of tablet.

step_by_step.step: Calculate total moles of HCl added
Calculate moles of excess HCl from back-titration
Calculate moles of HCl neutralized by antacid
step_by_step.explanation: Total HCl - excess HCl = HCl reacted with antacid
Divide moles neutralized by tablet mass

worked_examples.concept_applied: Back-titration determines capacity of substances that react slowly or incompletely with direct titration.

worked_examples.title: Dilution Correction Calculation
worked_examples.problem: A 10.00 mL sample of concentrated vinegar is diluted to 100.0 mL in a volumetric flask. A 25.00 mL aliquot of the diluted solution requires 24.35 mL of 0.1015 M NaOH for titration. Calculate the concentration of acetic acid in the original vinegar.

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply diluted concentration by dilution factor
Acetic acid molar mass = 60.05 g/mol; % w/v = (g/100 mL) × 100%
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{g/L} = 0.9884 \text{ mol/L} \times 60.05 \text{ g/mol} = 59.35 \text{ g/L} = 5.935 \text{ g/100 mL} = 5.94\% \text{ w/v}$

worked_examples.final_answer: Original vinegar concentration: 0.9884 M acetic acid (or 5.94% w/v)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Dilution corrections require multiplying titration result by dilution factor to obtain original concentration.

worked_examples.title: Percent Purity Calculation
worked_examples.problem: A 0.500 g sample of impure potassium hydrogen phthalate (KHP, $\text{KHC}_8\text{H}_4\text{O}_4$, molar mass 204.22 g/mol) is titrated with 0.100 M NaOH. The endpoint requires 24.35 mL of NaOH. Calculate the percent purity of the KHP sample. Reaction: $\text{KHC}_8\text{H}_4\text{O}_4 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{KNaC}_8\text{H}_4\text{O}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

step_by_step.explanation: 1 mol KHP reacts with 1 mol NaOH

worked_examples.concept_applied: Titration determines purity by comparing mass of pure substance (from titration) to total sample mass.

sections.introduction: Redox titrations involve electron transfer rather than proton transfer, but the calculation principles remain the same: stoichiometric equivalence at the endpoint enables concentration determination. The key difference is balancing redox equations to determine electron-transfer stoichiometry.

key_concept.definition: Redox titration calculations use the same fundamental equation as acid-base titrations, but stoichiometric ratios are based on electron transfer rather than proton transfer, determined from balanced redox equations.
key_concept.context: Common redox titrants include $\text{KMnO}_4$ (permanganate), $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$ (dichromate), and $\text{I}_2$ (iodine); each has characteristic stoichiometry based on oxidation state changes.

categories.description: $\text{MnO}_4^-$ is reduced from Mn(VII) to Mn(II) in acidic solution, accepting 5 electrons
categories.detailed_mechanism: $\text{MnO}_4^- + 8\text{H}^+ + 5\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$; 1 mol $\text{MnO}_4^-$ reacts with 5 mol of 1-electron reductants (e.g., $\text{Fe}^{2+}$)

examples.explanation: $\text{MnO}_4^- + 5\text{Fe}^{2+} + 8\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + 5\text{Fe}^{3+} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$; ratio: 1:5
$2\text{MnO}_4^- + 5\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-} + 16\text{H}^+ \rightarrow 2\text{Mn}^{2+} + 10\text{CO}_2 + 8\text{H}_2\text{O}$; ratio: 2:5

categories.description: $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}$ is reduced from Cr(VI) to Cr(III), accepting 6 electrons per dichromate ion
categories.detailed_mechanism: $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 14\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^- \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$; 1 mol $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}$ reacts with 6 mol of 1-electron reductants

examples.explanation: $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 6\text{Fe}^{2+} + 14\text{H}^+ \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 6\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$; ratio: 1:6

categories.description: Iodine ($\text{I}_2$) is reduced to iodide ($\text{I}^-$), or iodide is oxidized to iodine, with 2-electron transfer
categories.detailed_mechanism: $\text{I}_2 + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow 2\text{I}^-$; 1 mol $\text{I}_2$ reacts with 2 mol of 1-electron reductants (e.g., $\text{S}_2\text{O}_3^{2-}$)

examples.explanation: $\text{I}_2 + 2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3^{2-} \rightarrow 2\text{I}^- + \text{S}_4\text{O}_6^{2-}$; ratio: 1:2
Ascorbic acid reduces $\text{I}_2$ to $\text{I}^-$; 1:1 stoichiometry (ascorbic acid is 2-electron reductant)

worked_examples.title: Permanganate Titration of Iron
worked_examples.problem: A 25.00 mL sample of $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ solution is titrated with 0.0200 M $\text{KMnO}_4$ in acidic solution. The endpoint requires 18.35 mL of $\text{KMnO}_4$. Calculate $[\text{Fe}^{2+}]$. Reaction: $\text{MnO}_4^- + 5\text{Fe}^{2+} + 8\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + 5\text{Fe}^{3+} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$

step_by_step.explanation: From balanced equation: 1 mol $\text{MnO}_4^-$ reacts with 5 mol $\text{Fe}^{2+}$
$n = M \times V$; convert mL to L

step_by_step.step: Use mole ratio to find moles of $\text{Fe}^{2+}$
step_by_step.calculation: $n_{\text{Fe}^{2+}} = 3.670 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol MnO}_4^- \times \dfrac{5 \text{ mol Fe}^{2+}}{1 \text{ mol MnO}_4^-} = 1.835 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Redox titrations use electron-transfer stoichiometry; mole ratios based on balanced redox equation.

worked_examples.title: Iodometric Titration of Vitamin C
worked_examples.problem: A vitamin C tablet is dissolved and titrated with 0.0500 M $\text{I}_2$ solution. The endpoint requires 28.40 mL of $\text{I}_2$. Calculate the mass of ascorbic acid ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_8\text{O}_6$, molar mass 176.12 g/mol) in the tablet. Reaction: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_8\text{O}_6 + \text{I}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_6\text{O}_6 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{I}^-$

step_by_step.explanation: From balanced equation: 1 mol ascorbic acid reacts with 1 mol $\text{I}_2$
1 mol $\text{I}_2$ reacts with 1 mol ascorbic acid

worked_examples.final_answer: Mass of ascorbic acid in tablet: 250 mg (or 0.250 g)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Iodometric titrations determine reducing agents like vitamin C; 1:1 stoichiometry for ascorbic acid-$\text{I}_2$ reaction.

sections.section_title: Error Analysis and Precision in Titration
sections.key_concept: Understanding sources of error and methods for assessing precision is essential for reliable titration results. Multiple trials, statistical analysis, and error propagation analysis ensure data quality.

classifications.value: Multiple titrations assess precision and identify outliers
characteristics: Accept results concordant within ±0.10 mL (or specified tolerance)
classifications.behavior: Concordant results indicate reliable technique; wide spread suggests technique problems or sample heterogeneity

examples.explanation: Range = 0.10 mL; concordant; average = 22.45 mL
23.10 mL is outlier (differs by >0.50 mL); discard; average 22.45 and 22.50 = 22.48 mL

classifications.value: Proper significant figure application reflects measurement precision
characteristics: Buret readings: ±0.02 mL (4 sig figs for ~20-25 mL volumes)
Molarity of standard: typically 3-4 sig figs
Final concentration: match least precise measurement
classifications.behavior: Report results with appropriate precision; avoid false precision from calculator display

examples.process: 0.100 M NaOH (3 sig figs) × 22.45 mL (4 sig figs)
examples.explanation: Result limited to 3 sig figs: report as 0.0912 M, not 0.09116 M

classifications.value: Different error types require different mitigation strategies
characteristics: Systematic errors: consistent bias (e.g., mis-calibrated buret, wrong indicator); affect accuracy
Random errors: scatter in replicate measurements; affect precision; reduced by averaging
classifications.behavior: Systematic errors identified by standardization against known standards; random errors quantified by standard deviation

examples.explanation: Systematic: all volumes read 0.05 mL high; correct by calibration or apply correction factor
Random: different operators stop at slightly different points; reduce by averaging multiple trials

classifications.value: Compare experimental result to known or theoretical value
characteristics: Acceptable error depends on application (typically <1-2% for precise titrations)
classifications.behavior: High percent error suggests systematic error; investigate calibration, technique, or reagent purity

examples.explanation: Experimental: 0.0995 M; Certified: 0.1000 M; % error = $\dfrac{|0.0995 - 0.1000|}{0.1000} \times 100\% = 0.5\%$; acceptable

worked_examples.title: Assessing Precision from Replicate Titrations
worked_examples.problem: A student performs four titrations with volumes: 24.35, 24.40, 24.30, and 24.85 mL. Determine which values are concordant, calculate the average volume to use for calculations, and assess the precision.

step_by_step.explanation: Look for values that differ significantly from the cluster
step_by_step.calculation: 24.35, 24.40, 24.30 are close (range 0.10 mL); 24.85 differs by ~0.50 mL

step_by_step.explanation: Accept values within ±0.10 mL of each other
Range of concordant values = 24.40 - 24.30 = 0.10 mL; acceptable for student titration

worked_examples.final_answer: Concordant volumes: 24.35, 24.40, 24.30 mL; Average: 24.35 mL; Precision: ±0.05 mL (acceptable)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Replicate titrations assess precision; outliers discarded; average of concordant trials used for calculations.

worked_examples.title: Percent Error Calculation
worked_examples.problem: A student determines the concentration of HCl as 0.0912 M by titration. The actual concentration (from standardization) is 0.0900 M. Calculate the percent error and assess whether the result is acceptable (acceptable error <2%).
worked_examples.final_answer: Percent error: 1.33%; result is acceptable (<2% threshold)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Percent error quantifies accuracy; comparison to threshold determines acceptability of titration results.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstanding stoichiometry, misreading volumes, or misapplying dilution factors leads to inaccurate titration results. Systematic approaches and error awareness ensure reliable analysis.

classifications.value: Using 1:1 ratio for reactions with different stoichiometry
classifications.behavior: Always write and balance the chemical equation first; use coefficients for mole ratios

examples.explanation: Wrong: $M_{\text{acid}}V_{\text{acid}} = M_{\text{base}}V_{\text{base}}$; Correct: $M_{\text{acid}}V_{\text{acid}} \times 2 = M_{\text{base}}V_{\text{base}}$

classifications.value: Incorrect volume readings due to parallax or estimation errors
classifications.behavior: Read at eye level; estimate to ±0.02 mL for burets; record all certain digits plus one estimated digit

examples.explanation: Wrong: 24.3 mL; Correct: 24.35 mL (estimate between 0.1 mL marks)

classifications.value: Forgetting to account for sample dilution before titration
characteristics: Reporting concentration of diluted sample as original
classifications.behavior: Track all dilution steps; multiply titration result by total dilution factor to obtain original concentration

examples.explanation: If 10 mL vinegar diluted to 100 mL, then 25 mL aliquot titrated: dilution factor = $(100/10) \times (100/25) = 40$; multiply titration result by 40

classifications.value: Stopping titration too early or overshooting the endpoint
classifications.behavior: Add titrant dropwise near endpoint; swirl continuously; stop at first permanent color change; use white background

examples.explanation: Endpoint is first pale pink that persists for 30 seconds; deeper pink indicates overshoot

classifications.value: Reporting too many or too few significant figures
characteristics: Loss of precision from premature rounding
classifications.behavior: Match sig figs to least precise measurement; typically 3-4 sig figs for titration results

examples.process: 0.100 M (3 sig figs) × 22.45 mL (4 sig figs)
examples.explanation: Result limited to 3 sig figs: report as 0.0912 M, not 0.09116 M

worked_examples.title: Error Analysis: Stoichiometric Ratio
worked_examples.problem: A student calculates the concentration of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ using $M_{\text{acid}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{base}}V_{\text{base}}}{V_{\text{acid}}}$ for a titration with NaOH. The calculated concentration is 0.0600 M, but the correct value is 0.120 M. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation: Student used 1:1 stoichiometric ratio; $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is diprotic (2 $\text{H}^+$ per molecule)
Correct value is twice the student's value due to 2:1 ratio

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: used 1:1 ratio instead of 1:2; correct $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ concentration = 0.120 M
worked_examples.concept_applied: Stoichiometric ratios from balanced equations are essential for accurate titration calculations.

worked_examples.title: Titration Calculation Checklist
worked_examples.problem: Outline a systematic approach for titration calculations.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify stoichiometric coefficients for mole ratio
Ensure consistent units for molarity calculations
$M_{\text{analyte}} = n_{\text{analyte}}/V_{\text{analyte}}$; apply dilution factors if needed
Match precision to least precise measurement; typically 3-4 sig figs for titration results

worked_examples.final_answer: Follow 6-check verification: balance equation, convert units, calculate moles, apply ratio, find concentration, apply sig figs
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic approach prevents stoichiometric and calculation errors in titration analysis.

sections.introduction: Test your understanding with these practice problems. Try solving before looking at solutions.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems reinforce titration setup, stoichiometry, calculations, and error analysis.
key_concept.context: Cover solutions and attempt each problem independently, then check your work.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: A 20.00 mL sample of KOH solution is titrated with 0.125 M $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$. The endpoint is reached after adding 18.20 mL of acid. Calculate the concentration of the KOH solution. Reaction: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

step_by_step.calculation: $n_{\text{KOH}} = 0.002275 \text{ mol} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol KOH}}{1 \text{ mol H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 0.004550 \text{ mol}$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Stoichiometric ratios from balanced equations are essential for titration calculations.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: What volume of 0.0500 M $\text{KMnO}_4$ is required to titrate 25.00 mL of 0.100 M $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ solution? Reaction: $\text{MnO}_4^- + 5\text{Fe}^{2+} + 8\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + 5\text{Fe}^{3+} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$

step_by_step.calculation: $n_{\text{MnO}_4^-} = 0.002500 \text{ mol Fe}^{2+} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol MnO}_4^-}{5 \text{ mol Fe}^{2+}} = 0.0005000 \text{ mol}$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Redox titration calculations use electron-transfer stoichiometry from balanced redox equation.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: A student titrates 25.00 mL of HCl with 0.100 M NaOH. The initial buret reading is 1.25 mL and the final reading is 23.70 mL. Calculate the concentration of HCl.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Buret volume = final reading - initial reading; 1:1 stoichiometry for strong acid-strong base.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: A 10.00 mL sample of concentrated HCl is diluted to 250.0 mL. A 25.00 mL aliquot of the diluted solution requires 24.35 mL of 0.1015 M NaOH for neutralization. Calculate the concentration of the original concentrated HCl.

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply diluted concentration by dilution factor

worked_examples.concept_applied: Dilution corrections require multiplying titration result by dilution factor to obtain original concentration.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: A 0.500 g sample of impure KHP (potassium hydrogen phthalate, $\text{KHC}_8\text{H}_4\text{O}_4$, molar mass 204.22 g/mol) is titrated with 0.100 M NaOH. The endpoint requires 24.35 mL of NaOH. Calculate the percent purity of the KHP sample. Reaction: $\text{KHC}_8\text{H}_4\text{O}_4 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{KNaC}_8\text{H}_4\text{O}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

step_by_step.explanation: 1 mol KHP reacts with 1 mol NaOH

worked_examples.concept_applied: Titration can determine purity of impure samples by comparing moles of pure substance to total sample mass.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 6
worked_examples.problem: Three replicate titrations give volumes of 22.45, 22.50, and 22.40 mL. Calculate the average volume and the range. Are these results concordant (within ±0.10 mL)?

step_by_step.explanation: Sum volumes and divide by number of trials

worked_examples.final_answer: Average: 22.45 mL; Range: 0.10 mL; Results are concordant
worked_examples.concept_applied: Replicate titrations assess precision; concordant results (within ±0.10 mL) indicate reliable technique.

summary: Titration calculations are based on stoichiometric equivalence: at the endpoint, $\dfrac{M_{\text{analyte}}V_{\text{analyte}}}{a} = \dfrac{M_{\text{titrant}}V_{\text{titrant}}}{b}$ where $a$ and $b$ are stoichiometric coefficients. A systematic 7-step approach ensures accuracy: (1) write balanced equation, (2) identify known/unknown variables, (3) convert volumes to liters, (4) calculate moles of titrant, (5) apply stoichiometric ratio, (6) calculate analyte concentration, (7) apply significant figures and verify. Special scenarios include: back-titrations (add excess reagent, titrate unreacted excess, calculate by difference), dilution corrections (multiply titration result by dilution factor), polyprotic acid titrations (account for multiple protons), and percent purity calculations (compare pure substance mass to sample mass). Redox titrations use electron-transfer stoichiometry from balanced redox equations ($\text{KMnO}_4$: 1:5 with $\text{Fe}^{2+}$; $\text{I}_2$: 1:2 with $\text{S}_2\text{O}_3^{2-}$). Error analysis includes: concordance assessment (accept results within ±0.10 mL), significant figure application (match least precise measurement, typically 3-4 sig figs), systematic vs. random error identification, and percent error calculation ($\% \text{ error} = \dfrac{|\text{experimental} - \text{theoretical}|}{\text{theoretical}} \times 100\%$). Common errors include: incorrect stoichiometric ratio, misreading buret volumes, ignoring dilution factors, endpoint misjudgment, and significant figure misapplication. A 6-check verification checklist prevents errors: balance equation, convert units, calculate moles, apply ratio, find concentration, apply sig figs. Mastery of titration calculations is essential for quantitative chemical analysis, quality control, environmental monitoring, pharmaceutical analysis, and research applications across scientific disciplines.
